dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for breathing problems.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I can't breathe, like a 10 out of 10. Been like that all day.
Can you tell me your name?
It's Mark.
Where are we right now, Mark?
I think it's Richmond.
Do you know what day it is?
I'm not exactly sure.
You had trouble breathing, right? Can you tell me what caused it?
No, I don't know. It just got worse.
Your eyes are open, that's good.
You are awake and oriented to person, place, and event (A&O x 3).
Can I take a quick look at your airway? Do you mind?
No, go ahead.
Your airway is clear, and you are managing it well.
No signs of obstruction right now.
I need to check your breath sounds, okay?
Okay.
SpO2 is 56%, that's very low.
It looks like your breathing is labored-do you find it hard to speak in full sentences?
Yep, like ten words maximum before I have to take a break.
Resp is 17 per minute-some accessory muscle use noted.
EtCO2 is in range, 35-45 mmHg, with a normal waveform-indicating good breathing effort.
Blood pressure is 218 over 77-that's very high.
What's your radial pulse feeling like right now?
Pulse is 90-strong and regular.
Glucose reading is 147-no current abnormality noted.
No jugular venous distension (JVD) observed in neck inspection.
Your skin is normal in color, warm and dry.
EKG is showing sinus rhythm-no arrhythmias observed at this time.
How long have you had this shortness of breath?
Since yesterday. It's been getting worse.
Did you have any chest pain?
I would say it's a 10 for breathlessness. No real chest pain.
Are you allergic to anything?
I am allergic to Tramadol.
What medications are you currently taking?
Amlodipine, Metoprolol, Ferric Citrate, Renvela, and Dialyvite 800-nothing else today, though.
Can you tell me about your medical history?
I have COPD and Stage 4 kidney disease. I rely on regular dialysis treatments.
Have you used your inhaler today?
No, haven't used it. I just tried to breathe better on my own.
What made you call 911 suddenly today?
I just feel like fluid is building up in my body. This doesn't feel like a COPD flare-up at all.
On a scale from 0 to 10, 10 being worst pain of your life, what would you rate your breathlessness?
It's a solid 10 for me right now.
Second EKG-still showing sinus rhythm, no changes so far.
Blood pressure is 215 over 89-still dangerously high but stable for now.
SpO2 is now up to 97 from 56-that's significant improvement.
Your lung sounds are diminished at the lower lobes. There are crackles in all lung fields and inspiratory wheezing in the left upper lobe.
You're breathing at 14 per minute-noticeably less labored now than before.
CPAP at 5 cm H2O-O2 at 15LPM applied to help increase oxygen delivery.
Glucose is currently showing as 0-this is likely a machine error, we'll recheck if needed later.
Blood pressure is down slightly to 215/89 still, though stable for now.
The Continuous Positive Airway Pressure (PEEP) is currently set at 5 cm H2O to assist with breathing.
We're going to give you some nitroglycerine sublingually now. You okay with that?
SpO2 is improving further, now at 98-great progress from the low of 56.
No signs of jugular venous distention noted and no pitting edema in the lower legs at this time.
Do you have any pain or discharge in your nose or throat right now?
No, not really anything going on there.
Can I check your range of motion in your arms and legs?
Sure-go ahead.
You have normal range of motion in all extremities. You were able to ambulate independently on the sidewalk earlier.
Third EKG-still sinus rhythm, no abnormalities observed so far.
BP is down to 152/103-remaining elevated but some systemic improvement is happening now.
Respiratory rate is 14-maintaining at a slightly improved baseline but still effort is increased.
SpO2 is 98-consistent with the last reading; maintaining fairly well on CPAP support now.
We're repeating the nitroglycerine since shortness of breath is improving from a 10 down to an 8 now, as you stated earlier.
SpO2 dips slightly to 92 now-likely a minor transient reaction to the medication, nothing alarming at this stage.
Let's bump the CPAP up to 7.5 cm H2O to provide more support and see if breath efficiency improves further.
Blood pressure is now 152/103-still within our monitoring range, but improvement in both systolic and diastolic is still ongoing.
How are you feeling now in terms of shortness of breath? Still an 8 or 9 or better?
A little better. Maybe an 8 or 9-feels more like an 8 now than before.
SpO2 is now 91-continuing the positive trajectory and closer to normal now as well.
Respiratory rate is stable at 13-breathing is less labored and somewhat recovered in volume and depth.
Pulse is 80-a drop from 90 initially and it's remaining regular and strong.
We are going to administer another nitroglycerine dose now as part of the protocol to address pulmonary congestion symptoms further.
SpO2 is back up to 92-this is consistent with improvement in oxygenation as we continue to treat underlying condition.
Let's adjust the CPAP to 10 cm H2O in place-channeling further support to alleviate your breathing difficulty now.
How are you now? Still an 8 or 9 or better?
Better, maybe a 7-compared to before, I'd say I'm about half-removed from the worst of it now.
SpO2 is still 91-we're seeing steady progress in breathing efficiency.
Respiratory rate is at 13-behavior is normal but still something that we need to continue monitoring closely post-treatment.
Pulse remains at 80-ongoing regular and strong.
Blood pressure has fallen to 192/92 now-but still high and will continue to be closely watched during transport.
SpO2 is up to 92 with the previous dose and remains unchanged for now.
Blood pressure is at 196/71 now-monitoring this process to ensure sustained effect as we continue care en route.
Respiration is at 17 per minute-noting concomitant improvements in both function and effort.
Blood pressure is now at 211/84-remaining stable but improvement is evident for this marker.
SpO2 climbed slightly further to 94-your low SpO2 is markedly improving as we continue treatment.
I just placed a 4-lead monitor-helps us全程 monitor your heart rhythm continuously during transit.
Initially, a non-rebreather mask (NRP) at 15LPM O2 was applied to address your critically low SpO2 of 56%.
We are applying a transdermal nitroglycerine patch on your left upper chest now to help with pulmonary edema symptoms as guided by protocol.
Pulse is now 82-the trend of sustained recovery is now apparent for this marker.
Let's get some more updates now as we continue to monitor you en route.
SpO2 is stable at 94-good sign for oxygenation levels.
Blood pressure has increased slightly to 198/86-remaining high but still stable.
Respiration is at 10-perhaps due to the medication or improved diaphragm function.
You are alert and oriented (GCS of 15)-a very good sign for your level of consciousness.
SpO2 remains at 94-no de-stabilization since our previous check.
Blood pressure is now 210/87-still in a high range but our highest reading post-arrival so far.
Respiration is at 18-within the normal range as long as no worsening signs emerge.
A 12-lead EKG has been obtained-results show sinus rhythm, no STEMI findings, stable patent heart rhythms.
Your range of motion in all extremities remains intact-there was no indication of compromise or restriction present.
You can lie in a comfortable position for now-everything's prepared, and we'll keep you safe during transport.
You're already secured with five-point restraints and wrapped in a blanket-ready for transit.
Where would you prefer to go, Mark?
Any hospital is fine by me-just get me there safely.
We're heading to Johnston Willis Hospital now. We're all set from our end in both medical measures and transport.
CPAP is set to 10 cm H2O (PEEP) to ensure continued respiratory support during transport.
We attempted to start an intravenous line-however, we were unable to locate a suitable vein for access.
Let me just confirm-your dialysis fistula is on your left arm-so we took your blood pressure from the right arm.
We followed the pulmonary edema/CHF protocol 2-3 per field guidelines-it's the best course for treating your condition.
A final 12-lead EKG before arrival showed no changes-still shows normal sinus rhythm without STEMI findings.
I just saw you scoot to the ED bed independently once we arrived at the hospital.
ED bed rails are up for you-will keep you safe and comfortable as the medical team takes over next.
